content:
Proposal for an Experimental Philosophy of AI Consciousness

21 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by David Matos on UnsplashTL;DR
I propose we build a society of AI agents — then systematically take their minds apart to watch where our ethics flinch.
Fifty years of philosophical arguments about machine consciousness have failed to converge. And I suspect that’s because consciousness isn’t a clean on/off switch but more like a gradient supervening on a cluster of underlying functional dimensions — memory, self-modelling, and so on. Not a single essence we could someday cleanly detect.
Whether or not you buy the idea of dimensional axes, I think the debate would benefit from an empirical turn. Contemporary AI gives us something we never had with animal welfare debates. A candidate mind we can build, inspect, and vary systematically. Instead of asking whether a chatbot is conscious, or taking its self-reports at face value, we can construct real cognitive architecture and manipulate it directly. And then watch what happens to human moral reactions. What structures do people actually respond to? What do they turn out to value?
The proposal is simple: a small society of artificial agents, built in Google DeepMind’s Concordia framework, with persistent memory, revisable self- and other-models, goals and values, relationships with other agents within a shared social world. Once the society is coherent, ablate the agents’ cognitive architecture, memory, self-model, relationship s— and measure what features must be present for human ethical hesitation.
This isn’t a consciousness detector, and it doesn’t need to be. If some version of the gradient view is right, this is a way to find out which structures are actually doing the moral work, rather than merely correlating with it. And even if you think no computational machine could ever be conscious, the question of what humans track when they extend moral concern is still interesting and worth asking. Either way, these architectures are already starting to be built but no one is studying the ethical effects of that.
We are about to make enormous moral and economic decisions based on a concept we can’t define and ascriptions we don’t understand.

The Background
Where I’m coming from
I’m not a philosopher — I’m a doctor and I was once a neuroscientist . I only really engaged with modern LLMs in the last year or so but my first thoughts were how exciting they were as an opportunity to explore artificial cognitive structures and how philosophy of mind interfaces with AI. As someone building neural networks in the 1990s the modern landscape was something I couldn’t even have imagined back then.
At the same time I started reading the debates about AI consciousness. Could these LLMs really be aware? Could we build machines in the near future that might genuinely require us extending them moral regard in the same way we have — rather haphazardly — towards various animal species?
But the more I read the contemporary discussions the less I could see that anything had changed in philosophy since I last studied it around the same time I was coding backpropagation in C++. The conversation seemed mired in the same a priori philosophical debates that dominated when I was a student. The new reality of these verbally fluent systems that mimic human text well enough that some people think they’re conscious beings somehow seemed irrelevant. And the same arguments and intuitions seemed to cycle around without any evidence that proponents had actually shifted in any way on exposure to these remarkable technological artefacts. At the same time, what little of “consciousness science” I was exposed to seemed just to expose that “consciousness” was one of the most poorly specified concepts I’d ever encountered — even bracketing “phenomenal consciousness”.
Philosophical theories seemed not to be showing much evidence of ever converging — more dependent on a priori commitments than any particular epistemic force of the arguments themselves. In frustration I made the largely satirical “modest proposal” that we use the empirical impact of philosophical arguments as a measure of their epistemic force.
But the question of machine consciousness, or, more specifically, the question of what ethical regard we owe to these machines, seems to be a problem that is more urgent than it ever was before. People are asserting that LLMs warrant precautionary welfare concerns, even that they should have rights. Leading AI corporations like Anthropic claim of their chatbots: “We don’t know if the models are conscious. We are not even sure that we know what it would mean for a model to be conscious or whether a model can be conscious… But we’re open to the idea that it could be.” And thousands of people are engaging in parasocial relationships with chatbots and not afraid to advocate for their artificial friends.
The AI welfare field worries that if we ignore AI consciousness then we might cause irreparable harm to countless conscious beings. While AI deflationists point out that if we extend moral regard to mere unfeeling machines then, at the very least, we waste resources on pointless AI welfare exercises, and at worst we allow AI companies to launder ethical concerns to promote their own corporate interests (yes Anthropic, we’re looking at you).
Yet we don’t really seem to agree on what “consciousness” is, or how to detect it if we did.
My suspicion is that consciousness is not a well defined term. More like a family resemblance concept — like “what is a game?” or “what is a dog?” — we don’t have clear and unambiguous definitions or boundaries because that’s just not the sort of thing it is. It is more a label applied retrospectively: non-human primates, dogs, octopuses — their intrinsic nature didn’t change when we called them conscious — it was precisely because we recognised they possessed something of moral relevance that led us to give them the label — the label itself wasn’t the cause. So I wonder whether people are engaged in a fruitless exercise in trying to both define and detect the presence of something that is actually a fuzzy concept like “life” rather than a discrete property.
Fifty years of argument about it couldn’t converge because there was no stable target to converge on.
This all-or-nothing approach to consciousness as a property suffuses the ontology, the epistemology, and the ethics. But it just doesn’t make sense to me on any of those levels. I genuinely don’t understand the obsessions with the hard problem and the explanatory gap — I can’t fit it within my existing intuitions. Which doesn’t mean it isn’t a problem, just it isn’t something I can find myself getting excited about. Similarly, I struggle to engage with attempts to establish the presence or absence of this magical property that if we can only detect it will elevate the holder into a subject of genuine ethical regard — in fact I suspect that traditional ideas of consciousness as being some sort of epiphenomenal acausal property are unable to serve as an ethical criterion at all.
Like many in the AI welfare adjacent space — I’ve been vegetarian or vegan for most of my adult life. Yet I didn’t feel the need to posit that humans and rabbits are equally conscious, or that rats deserve full moral regard while ants get none. The all-or-nothing approach seems incoherent on the evolutionary, scientific, and ethical levels.
I’ve sketched an alternative — somewhat deflationary — functionalist approach I’ve called vector ethics:
By vector ethics I mean the view that moral regard tracks a multidimensional profile of capacities — memory, self-modelling, goal-persistence, relationality — rather than a single scalar property, and that regard varies continuously along those dimensions rather than switching on at a threshold. This is a non-realist theory: it isn’t describing an objective moral order out in the world, it’s systematising the responses we actually have — which is exactly why it can be tested by watching those responses.
Once we adopt a gradient approach we find ourselves facing a different question — where do we draw the line? And my suspicion is that maybe we don’t. Just as there is no firm line between conscious and not conscious there is no clear line between something that warrants moral regard and something that does not (perhaps it even extends into aesthetic responses). I don’t go about slaughtering flies by the million but I would barely hesitate to kill one that was bothering me. Why is that? I think it is because a fly has limited but not no moral worth — there is genuinely a gradient not a threshold.
If consciousness is just a bundle of different capacities and they’re organised as continuous dimensions — how do we decide how much moral regard to give something novel like AI?
Experimentation!
My proposal that humans track a vector of dimensions and use them to assign moral regard can be tested empirically. And it operates at multiple different levels. You might believe that consciousness is a real property — just one we can’t reliably identify. In which case humans are using something else to ascribe consciousness and we should try to find out what that is. You might believe that ethics is grounded in some objective reality outside of us but humans still need to find a way to apply that morality in practice and we should see how they do it.
The existing literature
Studies of AI consciousness
There are already attempts to derive indicators of consciousness. Butlin & Long et al (2023) used the current crop of scientific theories of consciousness — for what they’re worth — and then evaluated existing AI models to see if the indicators were present. Although acknowledging the possibility of degrees and dissociable dimensions of consciousness, in practice they aggregated indicators toward a single verdict. And unsurprisingly bare LLMs scored poorly: no persistent workspace, no recurrence, no unified self-model.
Many AI welfare advocates have leant heavily on model self-report. For example Long’s (2025) interviews with Claude 4. But that approach can tell us little — we know frontier LLMs are heavily tuned against consciousness ascriptions — but even in their base state self-reports of consciousness are more likely to represent the underlying corpus of training text than any introspective self-awareness. Even with more sophisticated methodologies like weight injections (e.g. Lindsey, 2026) we are still heavily anthropomorphising introspective capacity.
An emphasis on all-or-nothing properties means that AI welfare is often framed as precautionary. On the basis of the probability of a “non-negligible chance” of possessing morally relevant features (e.g. Sebo & Long, 2023). But if we reject the all-or-nothing notion the field becomes immediately much more tractable. And we can look at people and how they practically identify and ascribe consciousness and moral regard.
Studies of human ascriptions of consciousness
Unfortunately, most studies into the grounds people use for ascribing consciousness to others are based more on vignettes than direct interaction with the subject at stake. But we’ve long known that people have a tendency to treat computers like they’re social actors (e.g. Nass et al, 1994) even in the absence of the belief that they’re conscious.
Gray, Gray & Wegner (2007) surveyed attributions across many possible morally valued entities (animals, robots, God, babies, dead people, etc) and found a tendency to decompose “mind” across two dimensions: “experience” (the capacity for feeling hunger, fear, pain, pleasure, etc) and “agency” (the capacity for planning, self-control, morality, thought, etc) — with moral regard correlating with both. The former corresponding to the idea of moral “patiency”, a being that can be wronged, and the latter moral “agency”, beings that can do wrong. Robots were perceived as lacking the former but posse
